pages.text:
+ loos were painted blue, the ladies' pink. Tues 12th Aug
We shopped at Monastier Ecomarket + set of n.e.
though Lyons on a fast road. We pulled off the
motorway to have lunch at a bend of a road
in the woods. Simon left the beer bottles open.
Charles caught a wasp in one bottle. When Simon
went to take a final gulp of beer, Thomas
said "Watch out! Charles' wasp" + Simon spat
beer all over the table. We are about to turn
north up the Rhône valley.
    Beyond Givry we reached __St. Jean le Vaux__.
The pool is open until 8 in the evening, but
unfortunately they expect the boys to wear
skimpy swim suits. We have a delicious supper
of chicken + charcuterie salad. Charles is
terrified of the wasps.
    Wednesday 13th August
    Thomas bought bread in the village.
Yesterday the boys, John + Charles, were allowed to
borrow swim suits, but they had to wait to
borrow them today. They ended up wearing their
underwear / knickers instead.

Simon + Eve walked in the village + booked to
taste wine at 10.30 the next morning.
Afterwards, it was so hot, that even Simon
swam. So did Thomas + Eve.
    We went back to the Hotel du Raisin
restaurant in Givry. We had delicious the cold
herring + potatoes in oil, poached eggs in Burgundy
+ cream caramel.
    Back at the campsite we rested + swam.
Simon got tea ready + prepared a picnic.
    Thomas + John set off at 6.30 without a map.
The rest of us nearly didn't go because Charles
lost his shoes. Finally we found them beyond where
his out-door bed had been. William stayed reading
(so that he wouldn't have to put his lens in) +
Simon, Peter + Eve + Charles walked up into the
vinyards to the north of St. Jean. It was a beautiful
walk. We had supper near woods + watched the clouds
+ looked at old crosses. Peter found a dead snake in the
road.
    When we got back, we had apple tart +
black berries which John had picked for us.
    __Thursday 14th August__
    More trouble at the pool. John + Charles are wearing
knickers + Thomas is not swimming. We left John +

Charles swimming, + went to taste wine at Michel Isaie. We paid for 60 bottles. At the camp we lunched, rested + swam. After tea, Thomas, William + Peter read. Simon, Eve John + Charles [?bought charcuteries] walked up by water troughs + saw a bird farm. It gradually dropped more + more rain drops ... + then poured down. Peter + Eve read in the tent while the others went out in the van to pick up the Charcuterie + wine. John + Charles were given a tour of the caves.

	For supper we had pâté + Thomas + John fetched hot chips from the café at the acceuil, which were delicious.

__Friday 15th August.__
	Simon gave me tea at 8. We left at 10.10. We have headed up the pay motorway past Reims. We happened to stop at the same Aires 3 times with another family - There is about 7 hours' driving.

	After circling in a spiral for what seemed ages, we finally found La Belle Pêche *** at Hames-Boucres. It's a bit __old + tired__, full of families, nice dogs, friendly people + gnomes in their fenced-in [?playground] gardens. The café has happy music

pages.sketch_desc: A hand-drawn outline map of France with regional labels. A pink/red route line connects campsite markers (small triangles) starting in the Picardy region in the north, running south through Amiens, the Paris/Île-de-France area, a stop in the Loire Valley area, then Bourges, continuing south-west through Auvergne to Conques, then east to the Rhône area (Tampiny), then north up through Chalon of Saône back towards the starting point in Picardy. Title at bottom reads 'FRANCE 1997'.
pages.text:
[Hand-drawn map of France with regions labelled and a pink route line marked with campsite triangle symbols. Labels on the map: Picardy, Amiens, Ile-de-France, Normandy, Paris [?Avril], Champagne, Lorraine, Alsace, Brittany, Loire Valley, Burgundy, Franche-Comté, Poitou/Charentes, Limousin, Auvergne, Rhône Valley, Savoy, Aquitaine, Pyrénées, Conques, Chalon of Saône, Rhône, Tampiny [?Tain l'Hermitage], Bourges, Languedoc/Roussillon, Côte d'Azur, Provence, Riviera. Route appears to run from Picardy southward through Amiens, Paris area, [unnamed stop], Bourges, [unnamed stop in Auvergne], Conques, then east to Tampiny/Rhône area, then north back up through Chalon of Saône, and back to Picardy.]

FRANCE 1997

pages.sketch_desc: A rough sketch of a vehicle (described as a French ambulance resembling a Renault Express) with propeller or fan-like shapes around it, labelled 'French'.
pages.text:
like an English pub. Eve went to find out what was for supper. When I heard it was biftek + frites, I said we'd eat at the van. Little children keep cycling past with flowers on their bikes. It was a relief to stop motoring. I'd almost got bedsores on my bot. Pretty just on nines.

__Saturday 16th August__
Sunshine, but no coffee for breakfast. On the le shuttle we had a ambyplane, behind it was a Mercedes amnatance that looke like a Renolt express.
it was [sketch of vehicle with propeller] French

After coming through the Tunnel, we had a disappointing lunch in a Road Chef. We guessed that we'd be home at 3.45., but we had not reckoned on a puncture just by Oxford on the M.40. at 70 mph. Everyone helped change the tyre, + we stopped at the Oxford services to

check tyre pressure.

We picked up Tonic + arrived home at 4.30.

__August 22nd Friday.__
5.25. William, John, Charles, Simon + Eve left home. (Thomas at Greenbelt.) We didn't arrive at __Beauchamp__ until 7.0. because of a jam on the M5.

Peter played Haydn in his quartet. During "after cocoa" a boy played a tune on his violin by Messenet.

__Saturday 23rd.__
Charles has spent the summer terrified of wasps. William had a bad night because it's too short for him to sleep crosswise in the van. (His [illegible] new 7ft. bed arrived yesterday at home) We are visiting Gus King at 10.30. Wendy + the children are in Cornwall. Their house is lovely — half Tudor + half Victorian, moated, chickens. We picked blackberries.

Lunch in the field + Peter's concert went well. We went home after a cup of tea.

__Saturday September 6th.__
11.10. We motored to Malmesbury listening to Princess Diana's funeral on the radio. We had

a big lunch in the Jim Kirbys & David Kirby - 14 of us & Tonic. __Burton Hill__. **** Football, wasps.
   In the afternoon we went on a beautiful long walk in & around Malmesbury. The Abbey is gorgeous – Romanesque arches & carvings. A superb entry porch, & the Tomb of the first king. The tombstone of a woman eaten by a tiger.
   [strikethrough: Jim] Margaret & I watched everyone else playing an energetic game of football.
   A sumptuous supper - we all brought food to share. Much [?&] chatting.
   __Sunday Sept 7th__
   Straight after breakfast, John, Maia, Charles & Dylan asked to walk to the play ground with Tonic & David said "yes". Later we realised that they might [strikethrough: not] know the way. When we followed & reached the swings, we could not find them. Ten minutes later they turned up. Apparently they had walked the full circuit of yesterday, with John holding Dylan's hand when they crossed a road. We were horrified especially considering all the water + streams.

[postcard: three photographs of Malmesbury Abbey South Porch carved stonework]
[right margin, rotated, handwritten annotation:] [?amazing] [illegible] like + see the [illegible]

We played more foot ball & chatted & had lunch. The children fought. The grown ups washed up. We left, after a very good weekend, at 3.30.

Fri ~~Saturday~~ 20 Sept.

   Simon, Eve John & Charles left home with Tonic to camp at a pub __The Anchor__ at __East[?Ington] Welland__, to take the boys out for a walk on the Saturday. It was raining we had to drive out to another pub for supper, the Plume of Feathers, Castlemorton Common

   In the morning we had blackberries from the campsite on our cereal. Then we walked Tonic on a common where there were more blackberries. [margin, blue ink:] Simon [?Bumped] the bottom of the van

   At 1.10 we met up with Thomas & William outside No 9 & drove to an old quarry on top of the hills for lunch. William washed up. We walked to the top of the Malverns & watched hang-

gliders taking off. At one time there were 11 of them.

   After tea in the van we drove William & Thomas back to school & set off home, arriving at 6 pm

__Sat October 25th__

   All 7 of us set off for London at 10.45. We dropped off the church news leaflet at St George's & left William's racket at the Priory to be restrung. At [?20] 1.45 we arrived at the Lee Valley Leisure Centre __Picketts Lock__. **** It has had several smart new wooden buildings put up since the last time we were here. John & Charles are thrilled with the new playground. There is an ominous mouldy smell.

   We had lunch & Eve made coffee while Simon & William finished putting up the 2 tents. The reason for the mouldy smell was discovered — 5 large coffee mugs full of old, old black berries.

   We clambered on board the bus for the Tottenham Hale underground station. twenty

five minutes later... we clambered off again, when Simon suddenly realised that we were driving away from the sun. Fortunately another bus just happened to be passing. Thomas ran & flagged it down. Nearly an hour later we passed the campsite, & 15 minutes later we were on the train bound for __Oxford Circus__.
	Again we made a pilgrimage to Liberty's, Fabrics & lighting. We lost Peter briefly. Then we spent nearly an hour in Hamleys. John bought quirky stuff that blows raspberries. Charles bought a Porshe car. Peter bought a hand-shaker trick toy. We went into Next & Burberrys, bought a Coke & set off back to the campsite. Thomas bought tickets for a movie. Pizza hut supper.
	Eve put John & Charles to bed while Simon took T, W, P to the movie. Cold.

__Sunday October 26th__
	Gas wouldn't flare. Simon changed to the red cylinder. Heavy frost on the tents.
				The clocks changed, so we were able to leave at 9.30., catching 2 buses to 7 Sisters & a tube train to Embankment. A marvellous trip down the river on a boat in bright, bright sunshine, with an amusing guide. The boat stopped at Tower Bridge & we circled in front of the Queen's House before docking. It was very beautiful. __Greenwich__
	We visited St. Alphege's Church, with

captions: The ball is raised at 12.58 every day, + dropped at 1.00. precisely, so that boats on the Thames know the time.
pages.text:
its stained glass window to Thomas Tallis. The church felt rather like St. Paul's in the Jewelry Quarter - wide + open, designed by Hawksmoor.

    We went across into Greenwich Market - bustling, crowded, [?led] in by Christian Halstead. John bought a board game, Peter some posters + Eve a belt.

    We had lunch from some booths - hamburgers + Thai woked stuff.
    Then we set off up the Hill to the Old Observatory. Most of us went around the Observatory. Charles went round the slop

[caption below drawing:] The ball is raised at 12.58 every day, + dropped at 1.00. precisely, so that boats on the Thames know the time.

Flamsteed did endless calculations. John Harrison spent years perfecting a nautical clock, to help calculate longitude accurately. It was later used by Captain Coo[k]
    Simon + Thomas continued to look at the Observatory while the rest of us rushed down the hill to see the "Cully Sark" 1868, last of the great Tea Clippers which also carried wool.
    She had a crew of only 28 men. Carrying wool was incredibly lucrative. The wool cost £1000. Shipping charges were £4000. The cargo, back in England, was worth $100,000.
    Sail area was 32,000 sq. feet. She is 280 feet long.
    At 3.45 we started for home, on the Docklands light railway, after first walking under the Greenwich Tunnel which was built for workmen.
    We reached the campsite in time to

buy a coke & hotdog before the movie started at 5.45. "Contact".

      We went back to the van for Auntie Olive cake & hot chocolate before bed. Thomas, William & Peter set off to see another movies.

__Monday October 27th__
      (It is now Tues am. & John has just got this book out of the glove compartment. It is icy cold.) Monday was overcast. Sunday night was slightly warmer than Saturday night, but it is still very cold & the heater is broken. In [?the] bed I wear the goosedown jacket, a padded wristcoat around my hips, & an aran cardigan across my feet.
      We first visited the Imperial War Museum. Simon looked at the planes & SOE. Eve looked at the Trenches with John & Charles & Belsen, & code breaking. We all looked at the tanks. Eve forgot to tell Thomas & Peter what time we were to leave, but we all finally met up at the Tate with Robert Upex for lunch. The dining room was busy until 2.30., so we

looked around the wonderful Clore Gallery full of Turners. He was the son of a barber. Simon looked at Eric Gill sculptures - reliefs of the wind for the Tube station - female figures on lines.
      (The milk in the fridge is frozen).
      The lunch was delicious. Parsnip soup/ (with lemon in it) vegetable terrine. Silverside of beef & onion tart, [?for] lemon pie / mango sable. The waitress made a mistake with the bill, charging £100 too little. Simon thought she was the best waitress we had had.
      Charles & Eve looked at some old English paintings. Thomas & Peter went off shopping. We set off for Tottenham Hale. We stopped at Euston to pick up supper. William went to "Face", Peter (who missed the bus) went to "my best friend's wedding", Simon took John & Charles to "Hercules", & Thomas & Eve saw "Air Force One".
      After hot chocolate, we went to bed, but T & W watched another movie, "Full Monty".

Tues October 28
Freezing cold night. Milk frozen in fridge. After taking down the 2 tents, we left at 9.45. Thomas has washed + dried breakfast each day in return for sleeping in his own tent.
     We reached Madame Tussauds at 10.30. to meet Rob, Elizabeth + Cattarine Hungley in the queue. But the queue was 1¼ hours long so we left them to walk to Regents Park instead.

[glued-in animal cut-outs: snail, red-and-black frog, elephant]

We saw horrendous snakes, amusing chimpanzees + beautiful giraffes. John + Charles got lost after going on the way back from the giraffe bouncy castle, so we were late meeting Rob + the girls in Regents Park. (Thomas had been shopping + bought a fleece.) We searched for somewhere to eat, Topkapi, which was prepar[d]

to feed 10 of us shish kebab in 30 minutes. Afterwards we took 2 taxis

[glued-in giraffe cut-out]

through heavy traffic to St. Martin's Theatre to see "the Mousetrap."
     We trained + bussed back to the van at Picketts Lock, + drove home, (arriving about 10?) buying sandwiches on the motorway.

__1998__

April 4th
     Noon we left home for Northamptonshi[re] Simon + Eve + Tonic, Peter (nearly 15) John (10) Charles (nearly 9). William is with the de Fusses in Lyons. Thomas is already on an A level revision course for music at Oundle.
     We turned off the A14 + had lunch at the village hall at Naseby - good, savings, gr[eat] for Tonic.

When we arrived at Waddanhoe **2
we had tea + set off on the Nene Valley
Walk from the campsite, just a [?second]
of field below a hodge beside the road.
A very muddy trail. We reached the church
on a little hill. An old font. There were
pilgrim walkers. Down the footpath, past
the church hall, we reached the very
pretty ~~village, partly thatched. Very few
cars. The pub is the King's Head. Beer.
We saw a lovely manor house on the hill,
+ a Mill House. We wandered around the
watermeadow + looked at the lock.
    After a drink at the pub, we got
back to the van, walking along the road,
at 7.30. Tonic has been stung by
nettles + is much wiser now. He slept
the night in the van.
    April 5th
    We drove into __Oundle__ + found a

procession for __Palm Sunday__ outside the
headmaster's house. We sang hymns
at a few places + reached the church
which was welcoming. Thomas left a
message on the mobile phone to pick up
Matt Moon + him at 1.45.
    After ~~lunch~~ ~~a~~ church we had coffees
in the van + saw a bit of Oundle.
After lunch at the Coffee Tavern we picked up
Thomas + Matt Moon at the school. We have
set off through rainstorms for home. Matt is
leaving at New St. Sta. for Bristd.
    April 7th.
    Peter, John, Charles, Eve left for
__Beauchamp__ at 8.30. am. John is on a
music course. After dropping John we
went to call on Wendy King. They were house
tidying. The children played with the children in
the garden + walked. Eve had 2 cups of coffee.
Then Wendy led us to Robert Hammond's
house at __Taynton__. Apparently Gus King +

Mark Hammond are sworn rivals on the village cricket teams which share the same field. We had more coffee.

The field is so boggy that we are camping on the carpark. John's course finishes at 4. + then the boys played football + ping pong.

__April 8th__
    Having packed John off to music, Peter + Charles + Eve went to pick up Robert to shop in Gloucester. He found a good carpark for us. Then we went bad for a long, comfortable lunch with Liz + Mark + Victoria + Louisa. Mark's father wins prizes with his wine.
    It started raining as we set off to Beauchamp, + was a wet evening. Simon joined us at 8.15. for a late supper.

__April 9th__
    We went to the Waterways Museum at Gloucester. It is very cold +

captions: Inland Waterways by Alan Firth — Opened in 1794 with the completion of the Warwick + B'ham Canal, Shrewley Tunnel on the Grand Union Canal is remarkable for the separate tunnel for the towpath [illegible] main tunn[el]
pages.text:
Inland Waterways by Alan Firth
    Opened in 1794 with the completion of the Warwick + B'ham Canal, Shrewley Tunnel on the Grand Union Canal is remarkable for the separate tunnel for the towpath [illegible] main tunn[el]

[glued-in postcard/illustration: Victorian-style colour print of a horse towing a narrowboat through a canal tunnel, man walking on towpath]

old +

captions: Boat women's hats with elaborate frills & loops of curtain at the back.
pages.text:
has rained heavily all morning. After lunch back at the campsite, we have come in for John's end of course concert.

[photo: three mannequin heads wearing elaborate bonnets]

Boat women's hats with elaborate frills & loops of curtain at the back.

Relations were coming to visit [illegible] at home, so we wanted to return quickly. But it rained heavily all the way. We travelled at 30 mph with huge traffic jams & only reached home after 2 hours.

    April 24th Friday.
S,E,J,C,5·30. we left to camp west of Hereford. We went down the M5, west on the M50, around the south of the Malverns. We are staying at __Abbey Dore__, at the end of __Golden Valley__. The campsite is below the Neville Arms pub. We arrived at 7.30. Simon had booked supper for 8.15., but after making the beds, we ate straight away. We had pheasant & beef & treacle tart & custard & beers.

    April 25th
    It rained in the night, but glorious sunshine & blue sky at breakfast, so we were almost too hot.
    We walked to see the Abbey 1147, & then on across the __Golden Valley__ to the __Abbey Dore Court Gardens__, which were very pretty. They had a Teddy collection too.

The Abbey Dore building was started in the reign of Stephen 1135-54, on land which was then in the parish of Bocton, given to the Cistercians by a grandson of William I. The order had been founded in Citeaux in Burgundy in 1128. Its aims were simplicity & austerity: Farming rather than learning.
	Dore came from the Welsh "dour," water. Abbey by the water.
	At the Abbey was a woman serving coffee, who said that she had grandchildren at Blue Coat. She is

__Screen__

THIS IS A VERY FINE piece of Renaissance woodwork, almost certainly the work of John Abel, 1577-64. Surviving works which are

Jo McDonald's mother!
	Apparently Charles I's arms are in the middle of the screen, & the arms of the family who stole the monastery - Scudamore & another, are [in] place of the thieves!
	Beautiful stonework, but much of it has disappeared. They were having a [G]regorian Chant workshop day.
	We left after lunch: or rather we

places: St. Gabriel's church of the good shepherd
pages.text:
tried to leave after lunch. But we got bogged down - The publican arrived with a small Peugeot & also got bogged down, while making us more bogged. Eventually 6 men, who were on a llama-training course up the road, pulled us both out. Reports say it has been the wettest April this century.
     We reached __Blackmore__ *** campsite at __Hanley Swan__, near Malvern. Peter & Thomas played well in the concert for the official opening of St. Edmunds Concert Hall, Thomas did a solo & sang. We drank & chatted afterwards.
     April 26th Sunday
     We woke & drove to St. Gabriel's church of the good shepherd to have breakfast. 2 women led the service. It was the Brownie parade. It was an excellent family service - Charles claimed that it was the first sermon that he had understood, about the bad habits of sheep & characteristics of the shepherd.
     We drove down to the humsdens,

bought lunch at the ostrich farm, & ate it.
walked in the bluebell wood at Midsummer Hill at the southern end of the Malverns.
     Friday May 22nd
     We set off to __Malvern__ to hear Thomas & Peter playing in the orchestra for the summer concert. Thomas led the strings & Peter did a sax solo in the jazz band. We parked outside No 7. At 10 pm [?Hyon Murtagh] drove up with William to say that the medical centre had checked his foot, & thought that he might have broken a bone in the top of it.
     Saturday May 23rd
     Simon got up at 6.30. William came over for breakfast & Simon, Tonie & William drove off to Worcs. hospital at 8.15. They were seen straight away; William's foot is only bruised.
     At 9.30. Grandpa drove up with

Grandma + Aunt Helen. He drove us up to the Priory. We collected Peter on the way. Grandpa watched cricket with Helen + John + Charles. Then he looked at old cars in John's car books. Grandma + Eve went to the service. At 12 Simon + William joined us, + with Peter we watched Thomas receive the [?strong] prize at prize-giving in "The Space", the new [strikethrough] Winter Gardens.

     Gr + Gr had lunch with Helen + Charles at No 7, Simon + Eve + John went to No 8. We heard farewell speeches to the Murtaghs. We all left. It took 1½ hours to pack + load food at 8&, by 3.45. we reache __Dunar__. We had a campfire + there were midges.

__Sunday May 24th__

     Thomas + Tonic + Charles went for a walk. John, Peter, Simon + William played cricket. We only just

left for 11.30. church in time.

     In the afternoon we dozed. Peter flew his latest plane. Simon played cricket with all the boys.

     After supper we lit the fire + had hot chocolate. It rained a bit.

__Monday May 25th__

     Peter's Birthday. It rained + the boys studied. We played cricket before lunch. After lunch we walked up the hill + helped Peter fly his latest plane. It is a remote control glider made of foam rubber, polystyrene, tape + rubber bands. William took the plane back to the campsite - he can't walk far because of his foot. The rest of us went on a walk up to the house. Tonic has to be watched carefully so that he doesn't chase sheep. In the [e]vening we are having a lamb chop + sausage BB[Q]

__Tuesday May 26th__
     Sunshine when we woke up, but intermittant shows later in the day. We take a picnic & get on __Festiniog Railway__ at __Penrhyn__. We get off at __Dduallt__. It's a very, very pretty walk 1½ hours, back to Penrhyn. Oak trees, sweet chestnut, ash coppices! We had a picnic lunch. It's similar to the Cinque Terre, but no sun or Germans. I'd like to walk more stretches of the railway. We could see huge banks of purple rhododendrons & a nuclear power station. Most of the walk was in the rain. We drove back to [?Ainas] for tea. In the evening we had marshmallows & hot chocolate.

__Wednesday 27th May__
     Rain & non-rain all day. We packed up & reached __Machynlleth__ before lunch. There is now a diff railway which fills with water to balance each cab. Simon would like to make a solar-powered garden fountain & a waste-heat recovery system. One house with

double double glazing & 18ins insulation in the roof only has one radiator. I enjoyed looking at the organic gardening. "Our earth is not a gift from our parents, it is on loan from our children."

     We had a delicious filthy veggie lunch, then came home via __Woodpecker Hall__, Monmouth, but the Goodiers weren't there, only an Australia fly-swat.

__July 27th Monday__ S.E.W.A.J.C. (Thomas Train Crew)
     We left home after Tom's visit at 9.40. & headed north. We stopped for lunch in a field next to such a smelly pig-muck spreader that Charles & William were fed up & we moved into the village
     Heavy traffic on the roads, so we reached __North Shields__, __New Castle__, at 4.0., just in time for the queue to get on the Ferry to Bergen.

__July 28th Tuesday__
     The ferry was extraordinarily calm. We left at 3.30, but the ramp hit the bottom

of the van, & ripped off the water tank bracket. William & Eve went back to find the missing wooden board. We parked in a side street & Simon almost mended it. We looked around Bryggen - the old Hanseatic league wharves.
	We arrived at the campsite 10 miles out of __Bergen__, __Breistein__, * * * ½ at 6.15. There are little one room cabins to rent - a long bunk to sleep 4 people, cupboard, tables, chairs, shelves, no plumbing. There are bigger houses to rent - 2 bed/sitting rooms, kitchen, bathroom. The showers operate from a central switch board.
	The boys played football. Charles found a sand pit.
	It is amazingly light late & early - even the dark isn't very dark.

__Wednesday__
	We woke early, a shower of rain then warm sun. It rained intermittently all day, large, widely-spaced drops. Not an English drizzle. Mars Bars £1.50 (30p at home)
	As we got into the centre of Bergen, the van stopped & wouldn't go. Eve &

the boys went off to sightsee while Simon waited for the mechanic. He was only about 18, & soon saw a loose electrical connection.
	Eve & the boys ate delicious smoked salmon & shrimp french rolls, in the market, with a green sauce, & then danish pastries. [?John bought] a reindeer skin. Then on to the Bryggen Museum which shows Medieval archeological finds in the wharves. It is on a par with Stonehenge. It is a fabulous museum. The German Hanseatic league had an office there. We saw a shoe & a cow tether, fireplace tiles, gold ring....
	We saw St. Mary's church & then met up with Simon at the Hanseatic Museum. It was a very hard life. Boys of 14 were apprenticed for 6 years. They slept 2 to a bed in cupboards, tough initiation rites. They got cod fish, dried in the Lofoten Islands, from the north of Norway & brought grain from Germany.
	At about 5.30 we set off up

the funicular for a marvellous view of Bergen + the surrounding water. We had icecreams, then went down to the van + back to the campsite.

__Thursday.__
	There was hot sunshine when we woke up this morning. We set out for

Voss, through road tunnels, some of them badly ventilated.
	The scenery is beautiful. Deep fiords + lakes, snow on the mountains. Rows of fence posts + wires over which the farmers have hung hay to dry. Alternatively the hay is bagged in white bags as silage. Much evidence of cattle farming, but we have only seen two cows.
	The scenery is quite American with weatherboard houses dotted about the valleys. Barns. The bus stops have grass growing out of the roofs.
	We have stopped by a lake for lunch. William reckons the lake is polluted. He + Peter have been trying to fish with his rod. Charles changed into his swimsuit, but didn't swim.
	After a series of tunnels we went over a huge mountain pass. As usual, we were low on petrol.... + as usual Simon said, "I meant to get some before...."

captions: CHRISTIANIA S732 H + D. LADIES' SIZES: S - XL
pages.text:
On Thursday morning we stopped at __Oslo__ to buy knitwear. Peter bought a hat, Eve bought a cardigan "Christiania" is the old name for Oslo.

	At about 5 we arrived at Laerdal. There is a helicopter pad. We have camped right next to a marina. There is no ping pong. There are lots of silverburch. I am wearing a trousers, fleece + socks, but it is warm in the sun. Children here have large pedal cars.
	John + Charles have been able to hire the pedal cars.
	Heavyrain in the night. Warm rain in the morning + cloud down low.
	A delicious supper of prawns from the Bergen fish market.

[Newspaper clipping page — no handwritten diary text. Page consists entirely of printed newspaper advertisement columns including: property investment ads, investment properties in East Manchester (Blackwood), financial spread trading (CFD Index), Betting Coupes horse racing "plots", property investment offers, urban planters (www.urbanplanters.co.uk), loans and commercial property ads. A number "65" appears in printed form at top-right corner, which is part of the newspaper page number, not a diary page number.]

We left the campsite + went up a winding valley with spectacular waterfalls + rickety bridges, paths + ladders for fishmen.
     The valley opened out for a fascinating old church - Borgund stave church. It smells woody + of pitch. Dark + cosy. There are photos of funerals + christenings. In 1820 people were burnt to death in a stave church, so the gov't decreed that all church doors should open outwards + the church was modified. It was also 1800s laid down that every church should have seats for 1/3 local population. So the parishioners built another church nearby, + the old church remained.
     We came back down the valley + went to shops at __Laerdal__ + the salmon museum. In the 1980's salmon developed a parasite. The only way to kill the parasite is to kill off everything in the river, using a plant mixture. The rivers take about 4 years to get life back.

captions: Borgund stave church c 11.50. lovely smell of pitch. Dark + woody inside. Old photos of christenings, weddings + funerals. Only 2? out of 1000 stave churches remain.
pages.text:
__Borgund stave church__ c 11.50.
lovely smell of pitch. Dark + woody inside.
Old photos of christenings, weddings + funerals.
     Only 2? out of 1000 stave churches remain.

The solmon museum.
The salmon migrate to the North Atlantic to grow, but always return to their own rivers to spawn – N. America, Canada, Portugal, Russia, GB, Norway.
Temperature is important, + gravel beds. The Thames + Rhine used to be important salmon rivers.
	We went on a small ferry across the laerdal arm of Sognafjord. Through a tunnel to __Solvorngolden__ hot water unreliable. The boys are thrilled with the campsite. It's an evangelical hotel, with old fogeys (Wm's name for them) singing hymns in a chapel. A portrait of Luther. Ping pong, army slidey thing on a pulley from pine trees, + a large lake. Lots of Germans. The washing machine is a brute. Fell off its side. Simon + I were still ~~singing~~ spinning clothes at nearly midnight.
	__Sat August__
	Rain has held off, so I've hung the rest of the wash. The boys have the rubber dingh[i]es out + row across the lake. Lovely

lazy morning.
	In the afternoon John beat Eve at ping pong, William + Peter drifted in the rubber dinghies.
	Simon + Eve set off to walk 3 km down to the fjord to catch a ferry to see Urnes stavechurch, but just as we reached the harbour, the ferry set off. The wait of 1 hour, + early closing of the ferry service meant that we had to return to the campsite. Eve picked red currants on the way. There had been a row about ice creams.
	We are living well. We had reindeer pate for lunch. For supper we had smoked salmon to die for, salad, + red-currants on peanut butter on bread/rye bread for pudding.
	It's very good having the peanut butter to fill people up in the evening.
	Simon is finding the weather too cold.
	__Sunday Aug 2nd__
	We woke up to a blue sky + decided to stay on another day, thinking we would visit

the glacier in the morning, & the boys could [h]a[ve] play on the boats in the afternoon.
      We drove to the glacier museum, whi[ch] was very interesting. 3,000 years ago there wer[e] [?sou] no glaciers, 6,000 the glacier was very big Everyone in this valley died off during the Black Death, 134[9?], except for one little girl. ⅓ people in Norway died. It was very cold in 1700's; the snow of 1980's are making this glacier big now
      We left the museum & drove 2 miles to what we thought was the glacier. We couldn't see anyone at the glacier. At 12.40 we set off walking towards it. We didn't get back until 2.50. It was ENORMOUS & [?one] one of the most spectacular things I've ever seen. The reason we couldn't see anyone was they were too far away. We walked & slithered & slipped & climbed over practically smooth granite boulders, sloshed through frozen meltwater streams. Peter & Eve both fell & grazed elbows. Eve took to sliding, holding her blue jean dress under her bum, & [?grazed] her wedding ring.
      The glacier was very high 130 ft? it

[h]ad lovely blue colours in the crevasses & the cave which the water came out [of]. There was a hanging bridge. Peter [?we] found ice in the lake below.
      __Nigardsbreen__ Glacier.
      We were exhausted by the time we got back to the campsite, but Ste[r] pumped up the boat & he & Charles went off rowing. After supper we were looking forward to a quiet evening's reading. Charles was washing up & Eve was reading aloud when suddenly John came back yelling. He had fallen off the swing-out-over the water, & banged his head on the rocks, pebbles under water at 8.30. By 9.30 he had a bad headache & was vomiting. So we called a doctor, who asked John what he had been doing that day, & what day of the week it was, (… as if John ever knows!) & she sent him off to hospital, with Eve in an ambulance, to __Forde__, 90 kilometres away. Simon & the boys broke camp in record time & followed for 1½ hours in the van. We were very well looked after,

captions: A model of two Edwardians on their Harley Davigon at the Solomon museum
pages.text:
reached our room at 3am. In the [?a+d] ambulance John had a pill up his bum + oxygen in his nose + stopped feeling sick + was fine. We were finally let out at 1.0 pm.
__Monday 3rd August__
	Simon + the boys slept on the carpark + shopped at a supermarket.
	We caught the 4.0 ferry to __Ornes__ + reached a marvellous stave church
[Photo caption: A model of two Edwardians on their Harley Davigon at the Solomon museum]
We could climb up into the gallery + see beautifully carved capitals. Three families had private box pews, one of them still lives in the village - Bugge. Stave churches are built with very strong vertical pillars

[Glued-in ticket: URNES STAVKYRKJE / FORENINGEN TIL NORSKE FORTIDSMINNES- MERKERS BEVARING / Kr. 80,- / 027]

We found a beautiful valley to camp in, with a lake (drinking-water for the village, so no swimming) views of waterfalls. There was alot of rain in the night + the waterfalls increased in size; they haven't formed clefts in the hillside, they just pour out over the rocks.
__Skjolden__,** [?too] above the head of [?Luster] fjord

__Tues 4th [?August]__		__Borgsdalen__
	We left in a drizzle + went up a steep road - 8% for 10 km. It went on + on to a high area, Very barren + misty + mysterious; a crew of a glacier. Stupid

sheep next to the road.          [?Helgeflahen] [?Steinlakmos]
      We saw the beginnings of a stream
+ followed it down through lakes until
it had become a big river.
      At __Ringebu__ we saw a third stave
church with a red steeple. It is larger
than the others + has wall paintings +
big wooden beads coming down the [changlier?]
It was being painted with pitch + smelt
very strong.
      At this point we left the lowlands
again + have come up to a very lonely
plateau. The colours are mossy green +
also a very pale green plant, like a lichen
bush. Noone lives in the area + there
is no farming, but a few lakes for fishermen
+ bird spotters - but not many birds.
      We have come to wild camp, much
to William's disgust. Near __Tryvang__
Mosquitos. Near Koppang. We found the
mosquito nets.
      Wed 5th Aug
      Rain in the night, a cold, wet start, but

Ringebu Stave church.

the sun has come out now.
Mistakenly Simon, + Charles bought culture milk (yoghurt)
(6 litres) which we are still working our way thro
inventing yoghurt puddings. Our other problem
is that Norway doesn't sell Blue gas, +

changing to red would cost £70. So we are only using gas to make tea or coffee. Simon has started growing a beard.

    We stopped in __Korpang__ to shop, & then stopped in a lay-by for coffee, where we discovered we had a puncture. Simon & Charles changed the wheel while Peter fished.

    Road warning signs include "rekverk manglers" and "fakhit damperers." What are cars here powered on!

    I saw nice birch bark baskets & containers for sale lots of wild flowers. The old wooden houses are beautiful with wood fretwork around their balconies & eaves, painted(off) white. Barns are rust red with white windows & doors. The housewives crochet patterns [sketch of window/curtain pattern] along the tops of windows. Many of the small houses/wcs/barns have grass growing on the rooftops.

    We followed the river down into Sweden. [cr-] There were nothing but trees as far as the eye could see. At the Swedish campsite __Holjes__ ***[?½] we saw signs of vandalism for the first time in Scandinavia - a smashed

sign post board & broken lights.

    We camped in a very pretty meadow. It was a pity that there was no river. We walked to a restaurant & had a good 3 course meal - smoked salmon pork/beef & apple cake for £87 for 6 of us. Showers cost 5 crowns. It was a long walk to the restaurants past rather slummy looking houses - summer houses or year round?) Pikes' heads nailed to shed doors.

__Thu 6th August__

    We drove on through Sweden, so many many trees, & houses in the countryside which were not farms. The Swedish houses don't seem as elegant as the Norwegian ones.

    We drove through __Sysslebäck__ to __Torsby__, where we had to buy a new tyre for £110. At __Sunne__ the campsite was too crowded, so we went on to __Humbletorp__.

    We have camped near a lake. It's very pretty. After a load of washing, the clothes line pole broke. __Humbletorp__ ***

Fri 7th August Rain in the night, but a good windy day for drying. The boys

[photo: Selma Lagerlof's house at Marbacka]

[text at top right, partly obscured by gutter:] went out for a [bro?] on the lake in the 2 rubber boats. After lunch we came to the house. Mr [Lagerloff?] wrote Nils & the goose [Stores?]. we bought a book & [slice?] fruit bowl. [Alonly?] house which reminds [us?] of [Mackintosh?].

beautiful curtains, proportions & oak wood.

Then we drove on to Sunne. At Sunne we went to the supermarket & Eve found [bluegas?]. We had a good supper.

__Sat 8th Aug__
Lots more clothes washing, & we hired a big row boat for going out in the lake. We [like?]

the campsite, it is quite like [Cassianoel?].

In the evening we all met up in 2 large tents & celebrated "[strikethrough: Crayfish] Crayfish fest", singing + dancing + drinking + eating crayfish. Very friendly Swedes & Germans. Some have come for 20 years. It celebrates the start of the crayfish season, though now they are imported from China & USA. We met a nice family from Hamburg.

Drinking is a problem, you can buy weak ol beer in shops. For stronger drink you have to go to a special fair. So many Swedes have their own stills at home & drink very seriously.

__Sunday 9 9 Aug__
[Sushinelide?] drove to __Sunne__ & bought a paper. U.S. embassy bombings in Africa. Simon telephoned Sven in Oslo, whose wife is dying.

The houses might be based on building with logs. [sketch of log joint] So many have a central bit, as if to provide a place for logs to cross over [sketch of log joint] ***[?symbols]?

4 30. arrived in __Oslo__ campsite. V. pretty. John picked raspberries.

__Monday 10th Aug__
We had a wonderful day doing museums. We caught a bus & tram to the peninsular where 5 museums are, Bygdoy. We all visited the Viking Museum, then Simon & Eve went to the Folk [Museon?]

& saw [Gol] Stave Church & [barns] & someone spinning. The boys went to the Ship Museum. Then we all went to the Fram Museum & the Kon Tiki museum. They are marvellously laid out.
	We had hoped that the boys could swim at the pool in the park but it was shut. We looked at [Vigeland's] wonderful statues, an obelisk of bodies, [?not seen]. He showed us a restaurant where we had a delicious meal of catfish. John & Charles are fighting Svein Stoesen.
	Thank you for wanting to stop at any house any friends for the jolly good time we had together. I enjoyed every minute of it.  Affectionately, [Svein]

William - Kon Tiki: Heyerdahl burnt the Tigris in protest at the Red Sea War

Peter - Kon Tiki
John - [Gjoa] about fishing & getting fish
Charles - Gjoa - Amundsen's ship, outside & smaller than Fram, captain's cabin.
					[sketcher] [?$11] [?waterfall] [?leads] [?beds] [?free]
Simon - Viking, shapes of boats
Eve - Folkmuseum, peasant's cottage,
	 Viking shape of boats, Fram - Nansen's life, adventurer, ambassador, saved refugees

captions: The Gokstad Ship c 900 found near Oslo Fjord The preserved wood was steamed & boiled in alum & impregnated with linseed oil. Oak ship, ash tiller 32 oarsmen, helmsman, look out. Burial ship for tall, 60 year old, chieftain.
pages.text: The Gokstad Ship c 900 found near Oslo Fjord The preserved wood was steamed & boiled in alum & impregnated with linseed oil. Oak ship, ash tiller 32 oarsmen, helmsman, look out. Burial ship for tall, 60 year old, chieftain.

[postcard photo of Brekatedralen - Nigardsbreen]

Tues 12th August
   We packed up & drove to the centre to see the Akershus Festning – built by Hikon [?Magnusson] c/1300 & Christian IV early [1]7[th], lovely tapestries, wood carved chests, portraits.
   Resistance museum, 3 men ~~[strikethrough]~~ executed outside, one called Rasmussen.

We rushed up to Svein's house. He gave us a drink in the garden. Lovely wood panelling & applied arts. His sister is a ceramicist.
   He suggested we go to the beach for the boys to swim from an island to the west of Oslo. [?Hovedøya]? There was a raft & diving board lots of families but not crowded. V. hot & sunny. A marvelous resort so near a capital city. He walked round the island with Simon, & then with me, pointing out the views & islands & rich peoples houses. His wife is very ill. It was very good to spend time with him.
   We came back to the van & had a cup of tea with Svein before he went home.
   We drove on for 1 ¼ hours to [?Fiskum]**
near Kongsberg.
The campsite is a pretty old farm, with old barns & surrounded by orchards. A rifle range & trains.
   Wed 13 Aug
   We came into __Kongsberg__. There is an interesting museum – silver mining/mint/skiing/minerals? Simon bought a stone with a hole

bored in it as a candle holder. The old miners wore leather aprons at the back — arse leathers — to sit on while they worked. In 1881 they had a German lift where men could go up or down by stepping sideways onto the next rung — Until 16[?00], 20[?–] kg rock could be [?lifted] — 20 m. by a teenager using a bucket & winding handle. He was paid less than the trained breakers at the bottom. In 1680 they started using horse winches. 2 or 3 horses went round, hired from [illegible] local farms. Each farm supplied horses to work 1 week in a month. In 1880 they started using water power. The same water was used to work 18 machines on its way down the hill.
        Safety binding on skis started in 1950s
We saw the ski long jump records
9 m. in 1810 to 209 in 1994.
        Simon & Eve went to the Baroque church, but Simon got huffed at having to pay so we walked out. Lovely waterfall in the town, river near bridge. John found drill noises in good mock-up of mine very loud. William noted a Czech or Hung. crystal decanter, used to pay ski-book royalties to a Norwegian after the war, now worth £100,000.

headings: Charles picture of Transport  Aug 12th Tuesday
pages.text: Charles picture of Transport  Aug 12th Tuesday
